data.title: Toronto Stocks Slip, December Inflation Cools; Canaccord Genuity Rises on Higher 3Q Profit, Revenue
data.description:
By Adriano Marchese

Toronto stocks were lower Tuesday mid-trading, the first trading day following the long weekend. Materials stocks were the largest decliner, followed more distantly by tech services and energy stocks. Tech was the largest gainer of the session, followed by commercial services and health services.

Canada's consumer price index cooled to a pace of 2.3% in December from a year earlier, according to Statistics Canada, thanks to lower fuel prices. Meanwhile, wholesale trade rebounded in the final month of 2025 with a recovery in motor vehicle sales, capping a solid year for the industry.

Canada's S&P/TSX Composite Index fell 0.6% to 32878.03, easing from lower lows earlier in the morning. The blue-chip S&P/TSX 60 also fell, down 0.4% to 1909.34.

Canaccord Genuity shares jumped 7.9% to 13.00 Canadian dollars ($9.53) after the company's revenue and profit in the fiscal third quarter were lifted by active metals and mining markets in Australia.

Other market movers:

Sherritt International shares fell 20% to C$0.17 after it said it is forced to halt operations in Cuba due to a nationwide fuel shortage.

Kits Eyecare shares rose by 2.3% to C$19.49 after the company said it expects to report first-quarter organic growth, topping Wall Street projections thanks to strong demand.

Trilogy Metals shares fell in Toronto by 12% to C$5.01 after the company reported a widened loss in 2025 as it continues to advance the permitting of its project in Alaska.

Write to Adriano Marchese at adriano.marchese@wsj.com

data.title: Bayer Expects Provisions to Increase to Nearly $14 Billion as It Aims to Settle All Roundup Cases
data.description:
By Joshua Kirby

Bayer said it expects to pay close to $14 billion in total litigation provisions and liabilities as it seeks to settle all cases relating to its Roundup herbicide.

Monsanto--the German conglomerate's U.S. subsidiary and owner of Roundup--said Tuesday it was proposing a nationwide class-action settlement designed to resolve all claims that Roundup led to non-hodgkin's lymphoma from people exposed to Roundup. The lawsuits allege that glyphosate, the active ingredient in Roundup, causes cancer.

Under the plan, Monsanto will make annual payments over 21 years totaling up to $7.25 billion. Law firms representing the class action on Tuesday filed a motion seeking preliminary approval from the circuit court of St. Louis, Missouri.

Separately, Monsanto has reached settlement agreements for other Roundup cases, Bayer said.

"The settlement agreements do not contain any admission of liability or wrongdoing," it said.

The resolutions, including litigation costs, entail an increase in provisions and liabilities for litigation from 7.8 billion euros ($9.24 billion) as of Sept. 30 last year to 11.8 billion euros, Bayer said. That will include an estimated 5 billion euros this year.

The company will likely as a result post a negative free cash flow this year, it said. The company will set out guidance for the year, and results for 2025, on March 4. This has been pushed back from a previous date of Feb. 25.

The costs of the legal resolutions are covered by a bank loan of $8 billion, Bayer said.

Write to Joshua Kirby at joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby

data.title: Wheat Futures Fall from 3-Month High
data.description:
Wheat futures fell below $5.4 per bushel, pulling back from a three month high near $5.5 on February 12, as improving global supply expectations pressured prices.

Higher production forecasts from Russia weighed on the market after Sovecon raised its 2026 harvest outlook to 85.9 million tons and IKAR projected output near 91 million tons, both pointing to ample export availability.

Additional supply pressure came as India allowed limited wheat exports to support farmers following strong harvests.

Easing concerns over winterkill damage in the Black Sea region, particularly in Russia and Ukraine, also reduced fears of crop losses after earlier severe cold.

Meanwhile, demand signals remained modest, with Thailand issuing a tender for at least 60,000 tons of feed wheat, but this was not enough to offset expectations of rising global supply.

data.title: The CAC 40 Index Closes 0.58% Higher
data.description:
The CAC 40 Index gained 49 points or 0.58 percent on Tuesday to close at 8365 points.

Gains were led by Dassault Systemes (3.98%), Unibail Rodamco (2.98%) and AXA (2.73%).

Biggest losers were Carrefour (-1.03%), Thales (-0.99%) and Stellantis NV (-0.92%).

data.title: CleanSpark Leads Crypto Short Bets
data.description:
CleanSpark is now wearing a target on its back. Among crypto-related companies with market caps above $2 billion, it carries the highest short interest, as traders ramp up bearish bets amid Bitcoin's choppy February.

Bitcoin (BTC-USD) dropped to $60,230.14 on Feb. 6, its lowest level since the FTX collapse in late 2022. That kind of volatility tends to hit mining stocks first. CleanSpark has 31.68% of its shares sold short, followed closely by MARA Holdings at 30.24%. TeraWulf stands at 22.28%, Hut 8 at 16.09%, and Cipher Mining at 15.40%.

Not every crypto stock is seeing the same pressure. Block has just 3.50% short interest, while Bullish sits at 5.33%. Coinbase is at 8.93%, and Strategy at 10.36%

data.title: Gemini Stock Tanks Over 15% As Three Top Executives Leave Firm
data.description:
Key points:
  • Gemini is parting ways with its COO, CFO, and CLO effective Tuesday, according to an SEC filing.
  • The company projected up to $602 million in net losses and up to $267 million in adjusted EBITDA losses for 2025.
  • Gemini shares are down roughly 86% since their IPO peak.

Gemini Space Station Inc. (GEMI) stock tanked on Tuesday as the firm parted ways with its Chief Operating Officer, Chief Financial Officer, and Chief Legal Officer.

Earlier, Bloomberg reported that Marshall Beard (COO), Dan Chen (CFO), and Tyler Meade (CLO) resigned from their respective roles. The departures were later disclosed in a Form 8-K filed with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) on Tuesday.

According to the filing, the executives stopped serving in their roles effective Tuesday. Beard also stepped down from Gemini’s board of directors. The company stated in the filing that his resignation was not due to any disagreement with the company.

Gemini said it did not intend to appoint a successor COO immediately. Many of Beard’s responsibilities, including revenue-generating duties, are to be assumed by President Cameron Winklevoss.

The board had appointed Danijela Stojanovic as the company’s interim CFO in May last year. Kate Freedman, currently associate general counsel and corporate secretary, was appointed as the interim general counsel on Tuesday.

Shares of Gemini (GEMI) tumbled over 15% in the afternoon. On Stockwits, the retail sentiment around GEMI improved from ‘neutral’ to 'bullish' territory, as chatter levels improved from ‘normal’ to ‘high’ over the past day.

Financial Outlook And Post-IPO Pressure

The leadership changes followed cost-cutting measures announced earlier this month. Gemini said it planned to reduce its workforce by up to 25% and wind down operations in the United Kingdom, the European Union, and Australia.

Gemini has also moved to expand beyond its core crypto trading business. The company recently obtained a U.S. license that will allow it to offer prediction markets domestically.

The exchange went public in mid-September last year, with shares rising to a record high of $45.89 a day after its debut. Since then, the stock has declined sharply, hitting an intraday low of $6.47 last week. Gemini shares are down roughly 86% since the IPO peak.

Gemini is expected to report its fourth-quarter earnings on March 2.

data.title: EQ Bank CEO says Canada needs to give smaller banks similar capital treatment
data.description:
By Nivedita Balu

The CEO of EQ Bank said its parent company EQB is urging Canada's government to speed up changes to improve competition and give smaller banks similar capital treatment to big banks.

"Canada needs to compete with a greater sense of urgency to improve productivity, to evolve away from complacency. That need for competition is more significant and substantial than ever," EQB's CEO Chadwick Westlake said in an interview with Reuters.

Canada's financial sector is dominated by the country's big six banks. EQB, Canada's seventh-largest bank, stands to become one of the last publicly listed non-big six banks if Laurentian Bank delists after it agreed to sell its assets, and Canadian Western Bank merged with National Bank last year.

Prime Minister Mark Carney's 2025-2026 budget sought to improve competition in Canada's financial sector by lowering fees, making it easier for consumers to switch banks, reducing regulatory burdens for smaller lenders and launching open banking or consumer-driven banking.

The planned changes are not yet fully in place.

Smaller banks and fintech companies have pushed the government to change regulations to allow them to compete more fairly and make it easier for consumers to switch banks.

Canada's bank regulator, the Office of the Superintendent of Financial Institutions, said it calibrates capital requirements based on risk. A spokesperson said proposed revisions to capital adequacy requirements, including changes to certain sector-based exposure, are out for public comment and not yet final. Once final, implementation would be in the first quarter of 2027.

The head of the regulator has previously said it is looking at speeding up the process of issuing banking licenses to facilitate more competition from fintech companies and foreign players.

EQ Bank offers everyday banking services at lower fees as well as mortgages and other products, but is required to hold more capital per dollar of lending compared to the big banks, limiting some capital it could use to invest.

"Help us be visible. Help us make sure we have the things that we don't have yet, like the same capital treatment. If we get that, we can get more money into commercial, customers, small business owners and Canadians," Westlake said.

data.title: CA: 2025 saw solid sales growth, higher dividends, and strong CSR results despite FX and Brazil headwinds
data.description:
Sales grew 2.8% LFL in 2025, with strong gains in France and Spain offsetting challenges in Brazil and currency headwinds. ROI declined 5.4% due to integration and FX effects, but cash flow and dividends increased, and CSR targets were exceeded.

Original document: Carrefour SA [CA] Earnings Release — Feb. 17 2026

data.title: CA: FY 2025 net sales rose 2.8% LFL to €82.1bn, with margin at 2.6% and special dividend planned
data.description:
Net sales grew 2.8% LFL to €82.1bn in FY 2025, with stable EBITDA and a recurring operating margin of 2.6%. Segment performance was strong in Spain and resilient in Europe, while Latin America faced FX headwinds. A special dividend is planned after the Romania sale closes.

data.description:
